channel.title: Old Books, New Attitudes
channel.description: Recent content on Old Books, New Attitudes

item.title: Introduction
item.description: The Project Historians of medicine and science agree that something pivotal happened in England in the latter decades of the sixteenth century, as medieval theories about the body or the natural world diminished in the face of new discoveries, new ideas, and a new experimental method. And yet, the reading habits of sixteenth-century English artisans, bureaucrats, merchants, and farmers tell a different story, one that this project seeks to explore. Early modern English people were avid collectors of medieval manuscripts filled with centuries-old texts related to medicine, astrology, agriculture, or craft manufacture.

item.title: Credits
item.description: Lead Editor Melissa Reynolds, Assistant Professor of History, Texas Christian University Student Researchers Student Folio Transcribed Jane Allinger f. 64r Fiona Corrigan f. 64v Camila Erazo f. 65r Hudson Hahn f. 65v Stella Lenzie f. 66r Kasia Love f. 66v Jonathan Martinez f. 67r Kylie Millar f. 67v Aiden Reed f. 68r Keilah Scott f. 68v David Smith f. 69r Andrew Stillwell f. 69v, top f. 70v, bottom Vivian Velasquez f.

item.title: About
item.description: Crowd-sourcing Dyngley’s Digital Library Old Books, New Science is designed as a student-centered digital humanities initiative, intended to introduce undergraduate students at Texas Christian University in the course Technologies of History from Cuneiform to Coding to both digital and historical research methods. Students undertake the work of transcribing Dyngley’s manuscripts as one of several “digital tools” assignments taught during the course, which traces the history of communications technologies from the invention of writing in the ancient Near East, to the invention of coding.
